summary: Gruber investigates whether any developers have actually been admitted to Apple's iPhone SDK beta program following a mass email sent to applicants. His reporting suggests that while a small number of hand-selected developers were given early access, no one has gotten in through the official $99 sign-up process yet. He attributes this to Apple's digital certificate system not being ready and an overwhelming response of at least 10,000 applications. He also clarifies that the beta exclusivity shouldn't be confused with the eventual App Store openness, and adds a notable detail about how AOL's AIM client handles the no-background-apps policy.
key_points: No developers have been admitted to the iPhone SDK beta through the official $99 application process; only hand-selected developers have access
Apple's digital certificate infrastructure for testing apps on actual iPhones isn't fully ready yet, limiting their ability to onboard developers
Apple received at least 10,000 applications to the $99 beta program, indicating overwhelming developer interest
The long-term question isn't developer program exclusivity but App Store exclusivity
AOL's AIM client demonstrates that useful apps can work within Apple's no-background-processing constraint by staying logged in even when the app isn't running
money_quotes: There are developers who've been let in through the back door, but no one has gotten in through the front door yet.
The big question, long-term, isn't how exclusive the official developer program is going to be, but rather how exclusive the App Store is going to be.
Be wary of claims that 'An app that does X is impossible without background processing.'
I don't think Apple could let more developers in today even if they wanted to.
Two sources told me that Apple has received at least 10,000 applications to the $99 beta program.
key_insight: The early iPhone SDK beta rollout revealed that Apple's developer infrastructure wasn't ready for the overwhelming demand, but the real question was never about beta access — it was about how tightly Apple would control the App Store itself.
